summary: Gruber argues that one of the most overlooked flaws in iOS 7's design legacy is the drab gray tapdown state for buttons and list items. He contrasts this with classic iOS (pre-iOS 7), where tapping an element produced a vibrant blue highlight that was both joyful and functionally superior for usability. While acknowledging that iOS 13 leaks have revealed features but no visual changes, he hopes for a more dramatic visual refresh after six years of the iOS 7 aesthetic. His core complaint is that post-iOS 7 design has been 'drained of joy' by prioritizing flatness over vibrant interaction feedback.
key_points: iOS 13 feature leaks have been extensive, but no visual design changes have leaked yet, leaving open the possibility of a significant refresh
The iOS 7 look-and-feel has lasted 6 years, matching classic iOS's lifespan, making a visual evolution overdue
Classic iOS tapdown states used vibrant blue highlights that were both aesthetically pleasing and more usable/accessible than the current gray
The Mac has retained more vibrancy in its UI (e.g., menu bar highlights), showing Apple already understands this principle on another platform
The fundamental weakness of post-iOS 7 design isn't flatness per se, but that it's been drained of color and joy in interactive states
money_quotes: In iOS 7 through 12, the tapdown state is the color of dirty dishwater.
The classic iOS style was both joyful and a perfect visual indication of what you are tapping.
To me the fundamental weakness in post-iOS-7 look-and-feel is simply that it's been drained of joy.
The classic iOS look-and-feel made it feel fun just to tap buttons on screen. I miss that.
It was both aesthetically pleasing and more usable.
Think about how dull, and how much less usable, the menu bar would be if the selected menu item just got a dishwater-gray highlight instead of a vibrant blue one.
key_insight: Good interface design should make interaction feel joyful, and iOS's shift to drab gray tapdown states sacrificed both delight and usability for the sake of minimalism.
